segments.title: Why the Seven Years' War looks like a world war
segments.summary: The trailer introduces the Seven Years' War as an unusually broad case for thinking about conflict on a global scale.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke opens by asking why the Seven Years' War is so often used as a starting point for strategic theory. He presents it as a plausible first world war and as an unusually broad conflict containing many different strategic cases. That breadth lets historians compare warfare ashore with power exercised across a global theatre. The opening is useful as a compact explanation of why one eighteenth-century war became a recurring reference point for later strategic argument.

evidence.note: The introduction calls the war a possible first world war and says it contains every kind of strategic case.

segments.title: One war, contrasting land and maritime strategies
segments.summary: The Seven Years' War can support arguments about mass armies ashore as well as maritime strategy across a global theatre.
segments.body: The trailer contrasts a land-focused reading built around mass armies and huge forces fighting ashore with a maritime reading that spans the globe. Dr. Clarke stresses that the same conflict can be used to make either case. This makes the Seven Years' War especially valuable for comparing continental concentration with dispersed maritime reach. The passage also prepares the larger argument that neither approach can be understood in isolation from the other demands placed on a state.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts mass armies fighting ashore with a globe-spanning maritime strategy.

segments.title: How strategic theorists use a familiar but distant war
segments.summary: A war can become attractive to theorists when it is familiar enough to invoke but distant enough to simplify into an example.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that many theorists approach the Seven Years' War with an argument already in mind and seek historical illustration rather than an open-ended lesson. By the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, the war's name was familiar even though many of its actors and details were not. That combination made it harder for an audience to challenge a theorist with awkward context or nuance. The passage is therefore a warning about selective historical analogy: a seemingly clean example may look persuasive precisely because its complications are less widely known.

evidence.note: The transcript explains why theorists seek a known war whose context and nuance are less likely to be challenged.

segments.title: Why grand strategy cannot be purely maritime or continental
segments.summary: The trailer cautions that a state may lean maritime or continental, but context prevents either approach from becoming a complete answer.
segments.body: Broad strategic formulas become less convincing once the particular context of a country and war is examined. Dr. Clarke compares strategy to economic policy, where effective choices normally combine different measures rather than pursuing one pure model. No country can afford a wholly maritime strategy or a wholly continental one. A state may make one approach its overarching priority, but it must still understand the other and remain prepared to exert some influence through it. The closing passage turns the trailer into a concise lesson about balance, context, and the limits of strategic labels.

evidence.note: The closing argument says strategic thinking runs into context and rejects wholly pure maritime or continental strategies.
